title: Reading and Use of English – Part 2
description: For questions 9–16, read the text below and think of the word which best fits each gap. Use only ONE word in each gap. There is an example at the beginning (0).

content.text: The origins of human language remain one of the most enduring mysteries in science, (0) since no physical evidence of early speech exists. (9) Although researchers have studied primate communication for decades, the gap between animal calls and human language is vast. (10) Such is the complexity of syntax that many linguists believe it required a unique genetic mutation. (11) Provided that early humans lived in increasingly large social groups, the need for precise communication would have become paramount. (12) Not only did language allow for better coordination during hunts, but it also facilitated the sharing of abstract ideas. (13) Regardless of how it first emerged, language transformed our species into the dominant force on the planet. (14) Not until the invention of writing systems did human knowledge begin to accumulate at an exponential rate. (15) Despite the diversity of the world's six thousand languages, they all share certain underlying structural principles. (16) Whether language shapes the way we think or simply reflects our thoughts is still a subject of intense academic debate. This fundamental tool continues to evolve today, as digital communication introduces new forms of expression and global interaction. Understanding its history helps us appreciate the very essence of what it means to be human.

example.text: The origins of human language remain one of the most enduring mysteries in science, (0) since no physical evidence of early speech exists.
example.explanation: Conjunction used to give a reason or cause.

questions.explanation: Conjunction used to introduce a contrast or concession.
Determiner used for emphasis at the start of the sentence.
Part of the correlative conjunction 'Not only... but also'.
Part of the fixed phrase 'Regardless of'.
Part of the negative inversion 'Not until'.
Preposition used to show contrast or concession.
Conjunction used to introduce two alternatives or possibilities.
